metadata.title: Automobile Finance
metadata.subtitle: Buying vs. Leasing: Making Smart Vehicle Decisions

content.title: Automobile Finance
content.subtitle: Buying vs. Leasing: Making Smart Vehicle Decisions

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Taylor is comparing vehicle financing options in {{STATE_NAME}}, looking at buying versus leasing the same car. With state-specific insurance costs (${{STATE_INSURANCE_AVG_TEEN}}/mo) and loan rates ({{STATE_AVG_AUTO_LOAN_RATE_USED}}%), the monthly payments look similar, but the long-term financial outcomes are dramatically different.

In {{STATE_NAME}}, why do some people pay
$100,000 more for vehicles over their lifetime—
even if they drive similar cars?

slides.notes:
**Discussion Prompts:**
- Why do you think car dealerships advertise monthly payments rather than total cost?
- What state-specific costs should you consider beyond the monthly payment?
- How might depreciation affect your total cost of ownership?

objectives.description: Examine the financial differences between buying and leasing a vehicle in {{STATE_NAME}}, including state-specific costs.
Compute the total cost of ownership for different financing scenarios including {{STATE_NAME}} taxes and fees.
Assess how vehicle financing decisions impact overall financial health and long-term wealth building.
Make informed decisions about vehicle acquisition aligned with personal financial goals.

terms.definition: The decrease in a vehicle's value over time due to age, mileage, and wear.
terms.example: New cars lose 20-30% in Year 1, about 60% by Year 5. A $40K car = $16K after 5 years.

terms.definition: Complete cost: purchase, financing, insurance, fuel, maintenance, repairs, and depreciation.
terms.example: In {{STATE_NAME}}, monthly payment is only part—add {{STATE_SALES_TAX}}% sales tax, registration, insurance.

terms.definition: A secured loan where the vehicle is collateral. You make payments over 36-72 months until you own it.
terms.example: Average new car loan rate in {{STATE_NAME}}: {{STATE_AVG_AUTO_LOAN_RATE_NEW}}%

terms.definition: A contract to use a vehicle for 24-36 months by making payments, then return it or buy it.
terms.example: Lower payments but mileage limits, no ownership, perpetual payment cycle.

leftPanel.title: "I Can Afford $399/Month!"
paragraphs: A student in {{STATE_NAME}} making $2,500/month sees a car payment of $399 and thinks it's affordable. But that's not the whole picture...
When you add {{STATE_NAME}}-specific costs like insurance, fuel, registration, and maintenance, the true monthly cost is closer to $800+/month.

highlightBox.text: ⚠️ Financial experts recommend total vehicle costs stay under 15-20% of take-home pay.
� Always budget for state-specific fees beyond the sticker price!

layoutData.title: New Cars Lose Value Fast
paragraphs: Depreciation is the silent cost of vehicle ownership. A new car loses value the moment you drive it off the lot, and the steepest decline happens in the first 2-3 years.
bulletPoints: Year 1: Loses 20-30% of value (drive off lot = instant loss)
Sweet Spot: 2-3 year old used car avoids steepest decline

keyPoint.text: 💡 Strategy: Buy a 2-3 year old certified pre-owned vehicle and let someone else absorb the steepest depreciation.

leftPanel.title: Shorter Terms = Less Interest
paragraphs: Longer loan terms reduce monthly payments but dramatically increase total interest paid.

36 months: Higher payment, ~$2,600 interest
60 months: Medium payment, ~$4,400 interest
72 months: Low payment, ~$5,300 interest

highlightBox.text: ⚠️ With 72-month loans, you may owe more than the car is worth for 4+ years!

infoCard.title: Being "Underwater":
items: Owe more than car is worth

layoutData.title: Second Largest Purchase of Your Life
paragraphs: For most people in {{STATE_NAME}}, vehicles are the second-largest financial decision after housing. The financing method you choose can result in tens of thousands of dollars in difference over your lifetime.
bulletPoints: Monthly Cash Flow: High payments limit saving for emergencies and retirement
Opportunity Cost: $100K+ could have been invested instead
Credit Impact: Auto loans affect your credit score and debt-to-income
Financial Flexibility: Owning outright = no payment if income changes
Life Goals: Vehicle overspending delays home purchases, retirement
Compound Effect: Every $100/mo saved now = $120K over 30 years

keyPoint.text: 🎯 The Goal: Transportation that meets your needs at the lowest total cost, freeing money for wealth-building.

slides.notes:
## Common Misconceptions

| Misconception | Clarification |
|---------------|---------------|
| "Lower monthly payment = better deal" | Lower payments often mean longer terms, more interest, and being "underwater" longer. Total cost and net cost after resale are better metrics. In {{STATE_NAME}}, don't forget to add {{STATE_SALES_TAX}}% sales tax to the purchase. |
| "Leasing is always smarter because you're not paying for depreciation" | Lease payments ARE paying for depreciation plus interest. The difference is you don't own anything at the end and can't benefit from years of payment-free driving. |
| "New cars are more reliable so they cost less overall" | A 3-year-old certified pre-owned car has already lost most of its depreciation but still has years of reliable life. The "sweet spot" for value is often 2-4 years old. Plus, in {{STATE_NAME}}, you pay {{STATE_SALES_TAX}}% sales tax on whatever price you pay—lower price means lower tax. |
| "Insurance costs are the same for all cars" | Insurance varies significantly by vehicle, driver age, and location. In {{STATE_NAME}}, teen drivers average ${{STATE_INSURANCE_AVG_TEEN}}/month (national avg: $250). This is a major factor in total cost of ownership. |

leftPanel.title: What Else Could That Money Do?
paragraphs: Money spent on a car depreciates immediately and generates no return. The opportunity cost is often the most overlooked factor.
What if instead of a $500/month car payment, you drove a reliable used car (paid off) and invested that $500 monthly?
At 7% average return for 30 years, you would have accumulated approximately $600,000.

scenario.name: Mia's Buy-and-Keep Strategy
paragraphs: Mia considered leasing a new SUV for $450/mo but instead purchased a certified pre-owned sedan for $18,000 in {{STATE_NAME}}.
After 4 years she owned it outright. For the next 3 years, she drove payment-free while saving $350/month—that's $12,600 saved or invested!

scenario.name: James's Perpetual Lease Cycle
paragraphs: James loved having the newest truck with latest features. He leased in {{STATE_NAME}} for $499/month + $2,000 down on a 36-month lease.
When it ended, he leased again: $525/month + $2,000 down for another 36 months.
Over 6 years: $36,864 in payments + $4,000 in down payments + $2,000 in overage fees = $42,864 with NO asset.

content.question:
In {{STATE_NAME}}, with {{STATE_SALES_TAX}}% sales tax and ${{STATE_INSURANCE_AVG_TEEN}}/mo teen insurance, what financial trade-offs did Mia and James make?

When might James's approach make sense for someone?

Given {{STATE_NAME}}'s auto loan rates ({{STATE_AVG_AUTO_LOAN_RATE_NEW}}% new, {{STATE_AVG_AUTO_LOAN_RATE_USED}}% used) and your anticipated career, which vehicle financing approach would best support your financial goals?

questions.question: What is "Total Cost of Ownership" and why is it more important than just the monthly payment?
In {{STATE_NAME}}, with {{STATE_SALES_TAX}}% sales tax, how much tax would you pay on a $25,000 vehicle?
Why is buying a 2-3 year old used car often the best financial strategy?
If you invest $300/month instead of spending it on a car payment, how much would you have after 20 years at 7%?

slides.notes:
## Answer Key

**Question 1:** Total cost includes all payments, interest, depreciation, insurance, fuel, maintenance, and what the car is worth at the end. In {{STATE_NAME}}, this includes {{STATE_SALES_TAX}}% sales tax and ${{STATE_REGISTRATION_ANNUAL}}/year registration. Monthly payment alone ignores loan length, interest total, and whether you have an asset at the end. A $300/month lease seems cheaper than $500 loan payment, but leaves you with nothing.

**Question 2:** $25,000 × ({{STATE_SALES_TAX}}/100) = calculated based on state sales tax rate

**Question 3:** A 3-year-old certified pre-owned car has already lost most of its depreciation (cars lose 20-30% in Year 1, about 60% by Year 5) but still has years of reliable life. The "sweet spot" for value is often 2-4 years old. Plus, in {{STATE_NAME}}, you pay {{STATE_SALES_TAX}}% sales tax on whatever price you pay—lower price means lower tax.

**Question 4:** $300/month invested for 20 years at 7% average return = approximately $155,000 (using compound interest formula). This demonstrates the significant opportunity cost of vehicle overspending.

**Additional Context:**
- Situations where leasing might make sense: (1) Business use with tax deductions, (2) Need newest safety features for short-term, (3) Job requires always having a new reliable car, (4) Planning to relocate/uncertain about long-term vehicle needs, (5) Cannot afford down payment for purchase.
- Personal decision-making should consider: total cost (not just monthly), {{STATE_NAME}}-specific costs ({{STATE_SALES_TAX}}% tax, ${{STATE_INSURANCE_AVG_TEEN}}/mo insurance), realistic assessment of personal situation, and alignment with stated financial goals.

takeaways.title: Know Your {{STATE_NAME}} Costs
takeaways.description: {{STATE_SALES_TAX}}% sales tax + ${{STATE_REGISTRATION_INITIAL}} registration + ${{STATE_INSURANCE_AVG_TEEN}}/mo insurance adds up fast

takeaways.title: Buying Builds Equity
takeaways.description: Ownership means payment-free years after payoff and an asset you can sell—leasing means perpetual payments

takeaways.title: Depreciation Matters
takeaways.description: New cars lose 20-30% in year one—buying used avoids the steepest decline

takeaways.title: Consider Opportunity Cost
takeaways.description: The money you save on vehicle costs can build significant wealth if invested over time

You'll analyze three scenarios for a vehicle you're interested in and determine which approach best supports different financial goals.

steps: Calculate: Lease new (36mo with minimal down)
